questions.prompt: Complete: "We ___ a great time at the party."
questions.explanation: have is irregular: its past is had (We had a great time). There is no "haved" or "haded" — irregular verbs never take -ed — and "has" is the present he/she/it form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I ___ a really good film last night."
questions.explanation: see is irregular: past = saw (I saw a film). "seed"/"sawed" wrongly add -ed, and "seen" is the past participle (used with have: I have seen it).

questions.prompt: Complete: "She ___ the message, so she didn't reply."
questions.explanation: After didn't, the verb returns to its base form: She didn't see the message. "didn't saw" and "didn't seen" keep a past form wrongly, and "not saw" is missing the helper did.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We moved to this town three years ___."
questions.explanation: ago means "in the past, counting back from now" and follows the time: three years ago. "before" needs a reference point (before 2010), "last" comes before a noun (last year), and "since" marks a starting point (since 2010).

questions.prompt: Complete: "He ___ a new phone for his birthday."
questions.explanation: get is irregular: past = got (He got a new phone). "getted" invents an -ed form, "gotten" is a past participle (mainly US English, used with have), and "gets" is the present.

options.text: She took the bus to work.
She taked the bus to work.
She tooked the bus to work.
She did took the bus to work.

questions.explanation: take is irregular: past = took, used on its own in an affirmative sentence: She took the bus. "taked"/"tooked" are wrong forms, and "did took" double-marks the past (did already makes it past, so the verb would be the base: she did take, for emphasis only).
